title: Monday.com vs Trello for Power users
meta_description: Monday.com vs Trello for power users who manage structured workflows with custom task fields and complex project data.
constraint_lens: You need a task system that can store structured project data with multiple fields attached to each task.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for power users managing structured project workflows.
one_second_verdict.reason: Trello fails first because custom task fields and structured board data are constrained.

verdict.summary: Monday.com wins for power users managing structured project workflows. Each task row can include multiple data fields such as status, owner, dates, and numeric values. Trello organizes tasks as cards with limited structured data fields. If custom task fields and structured board data are constrained, Trello fails first.
verdict.decision_rule: If custom task fields and structured board data are constrained, Trello fails first.

sections.heading: Best fit for structured project data
sections.content: You run workflows where each task includes multiple pieces of structured information. Monday.com boards store tasks as rows with columns for status, dates, owners, numbers, and other attributes. Trello cards focus on descriptions, checklists, and labels rather than structured data fields.

bullets.point: Custom columns attached to every task row
bullets.why_it_matters: Tasks can store structured fields such as status, owner, priority, and dates.

bullets.point: Table-style board structure with multiple data columns
bullets.why_it_matters: Project information is tracked directly alongside each task.
Workflow steps update automatically when task data changes.

bullets.point: Kanban boards with draggable task cards
bullets.why_it_matters: Teams move tasks between workflow stages visually.

bullets.point: Quick card creation inside any column
bullets.why_it_matters: Users start tracking tasks without configuring fields.

bullets.point: Flexible boards adaptable to many workflows
bullets.why_it_matters: Boards work for lightweight project tracking without structured data models.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: Your workflow only needs simple task cards moving across stages without structured data fields.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Trello when visual task movement matters more than data tracking.

items.fails_when: Tasks require multiple data attributes such as status, owners, and numeric values stored directly on the board.
items.what_to_do_instead: Switch to Monday.com to track structured project data.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: If your workflow mainly involves moving tasks across stages without tracking detailed task attributes, Trello may feel faster.

sections.heading: Quick rules
rules: If tasks need structured data fields, choose Monday.com.
If boards behave like simple kanban pipelines, Trello can work.
If workflows require automation tied to task data, choose Monday.com.

faqs.q: What makes Monday.com better for structured workflows?
faqs.a: Each task row supports multiple custom columns such as status, owner, date, and numeric fields.

faqs.q: Does Trello support custom task fields?
faqs.a: Basic fields exist, but Trello primarily uses card descriptions and labels instead of structured columns.

faqs.q: Which tool works better for tracking project data?
faqs.a: Monday.com works better because boards store multiple data attributes for each task.

faqs.q: When would Trello be the better option?
faqs.a: Trello works well when projects only require simple kanban boards without structured data.
